unit.name: Choosing model classes and running inference
unit.description: Covers task-specific model classes, architectural constraints, how inputs are passed into models, and how logits, loss, and predictions are produced.
unit.summary: The lecture moves from tokenizers to models and shows that Hugging Face provides both model-specific classes and AutoModel-style loaders that select the correct implementation automatically [ts=856s] [ts=960s]. The speaker explains that pre-trained transformers share a backbone idea but expose different task heads, such as sequence classification, masked language modeling, or base representations, and illustrates this with DistilBERT variants [ts=894s] [ts=947s]. He also emphasizes an architectural constraint: encoder-only models like DistilBERT cannot be used as sequence-to-sequence encoder-decoder systems, so the architecture has to match the task [ts=1057s] [ts=1086s]. The segment then demonstrates two ways to call the model: explicitly passing input_ids and attention_mask, or unpacking the tokenizer output dictionary with Python's double-asterisk syntax [ts=1180s] [ts=1260s]. Finally, he shows that Hugging Face outputs logits for each class, that binary classification still uses two logits, and that these models are ordinary PyTorch modules, so one can compute loss with PyTorch or pass labels to the model and let Hugging Face return the loss directly; predictions then come from argmax over logits [ts=1278s] [ts=1354s] [ts=1385s] [ts=1440s].

key_points.text: Hugging Face exposes task-specific classes such as DistilBertForSequenceClassification, DistilBertForMaskedLM, and the base DistilBERT model.
AutoModel plays the same convenience role for models that AutoTokenizer plays for tokenizers.
Model architecture constrains valid tasks; an encoder-only model like DistilBERT is not a sequence-to-sequence model.
Tokenizer outputs can be passed to the model either by naming input_ids and attention_mask explicitly or by unpacking the dictionary with **.
Sequence classification outputs logits over the label set, and binary classification is represented with two class logits.
Because Hugging Face models are PyTorch modules, loss.backward works normally for fine-tuning.
Passing labels into the model makes Hugging Face include the loss in the output, and argmax over logits gives the predicted label.

He also emphasizes an architectural constraint: encoder-only models like DistilBERT cannot be used as sequence-to-sequence encoder-decoder systems, so the architecture has to match the task [ts=1057s] [ts=1086s]. The segment then demonstrates two ways to call the model: explicitly passing input_ids and attention_mask, or unpacking the tokenizer output dictionary with Python's double-asterisk syntax [ts=1180s] [ts=1260s]. kp_catalog.name: Task-specific model classes and architecture-task matching
kp_catalog.description: The idea that Hugging Face exposes different classes and heads for different tasks, and that valid task choices depend on whether a model is encoder-only, decoder-only, or encoder-decoder.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because task-specific model classes and architecture-task matching formalizes how the idea that Hugging Face exposes different classes and heads for different tasks, and that valid task choices depend on whether a model is encoder-only, decoder-only, or encoder-decoder, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Sequence classification outputs, loss, and predictions
kp_catalog.description: How to pass tokenized inputs through a classification model, read logits, obtain loss, and convert logits to predicted labels.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because sequence classification outputs, loss, and predictions clarifies how to pass tokenized inputs through a classification model, read logits, obtain loss, and convert logits to predicted labels, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.

kp_catalog.name: Transformer tokenization outputs
kp_catalog.description: How raw text becomes input IDs, attention masks, and special-token-augmented sequences aligned with a model's vocabulary.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because transformer tokenization outputs formalizes how how raw text becomes input IDs, attention masks, and special-token-augmented sequences aligned with a model's vocabulary, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.
